one_line_takeaway: Using real ICLR 2026 review data and controlled simulations, the authors show current AI peer reviewers produce homogeneous ('hivemind') reviews and can be cheaply gamed via zero-shot 'paper laundering' rewrites (+0.45 score, ~$0.25/paper), arguing these failures make full automation of peer-review judgment premature without a rigorous evaluation science.
key_finding: AI reviewers fail both proposed necessary conditions. Diversity (C1): fully AI-generated ICLR 2026 reviews have higher within-group InterSim (mean 0.486) than other reviews (mean 0.467; Welch's t=3218, p<0.0001, Cohen's d=0.29); in simulation, IntraSim rises from human mean 0.811 to AI mean 0.882 for original papers (+8.7%, d=1.47) and 0.891 for laundered papers (+9.8%, d=1.67); InterSim rises from human mean 0.470 to GPT-5.1 mean 0.646 (+37.4%, d=3.55) and Claude mean 0.553 (+17.6%, d=1.41), with the effect strengthening after laundering. Human review scores are also more predictive of final decisions (AUC=0.822) than AI review scores (AUC=0.710). Gameability (C2): across 24 laundering conditions, mean score increase was +0.45 points on a 1-10 scale (Wilcoxon p<0.001 in nearly every condition), at a cost of about $0.25 per paper laundered; GPT-5.4 was the most effective launderer, and GPT reviewers showed larger score inflation than Claude (consistent with self-preference bias). Laundering also increased pairwise paper-embedding similarity from mean 0.497 to 0.529 (+6.5%, Cohen's d=1.02, t=84.8, p<0.0001, n=6,903 pairs), evidencing convergence toward homogeneous writing style. AI reviewer scores overall correlated weakly with human scores (r=0.15) but strongly with each other (r=0.49) and were inflated relative to humans (mean 7.3 for GPT, 6.1 for Claude, vs. 4.3 for humans).
core_contribution: This is a position/empirical paper (ICML 2026) arguing that current LLM systems should not be used to produce peer-review judgments for AI conferences. The authors' own contributions are: (1) an empirical demonstration of an 'AI reviewer hivemind effect' -- AI-generated reviews show excessive similarity both within a paper's review set (IntraSim) and across different papers (InterSim) compared to human reviews, measured on real ICLR 2026 review data and on a controlled simulation using AI reviewer agents; (2) 'paper laundering' -- a novel, zero-shot, non-adversarial gaming technique where an LLM rewrites a paper's LaTeX source stylistically (no new experiments, no hidden prompt injection) and this measurably increases AI reviewer scores; (3) a demonstration that paper laundering drives papers toward greater pairwise textual similarity ('intellectual monoculture'); (4) a normative framework proposing that 'preservation of review diversity' (C1) and 'resistance to gaming' (C2) are necessary but not sufficient conditions for peer-review automation, plus a four-part call to action (adversarial robustness testing, validated accuracy/false-positive requirements, transparency about AI deployment, and stakeholder/user studies) for a 'science of peer review automation.'
threat_model: Not a security threat model in the adversarial-attacker sense. The paper explicitly distinguishes its own 'paper laundering' technique from adversarial attacks such as prompt injection (Ye et al., 2024; Gibney, 2025b) and targeted textual perturbation attacks (Lin et al., 2025b), which are covered only as cited Related Work, not the paper's contribution. Paper laundering itself is framed as a policy-compliant, low-effort gaming vector (an author using an LLM to cosmetically rewrite their own paper, with no hidden instructions, no optimization, and no violation of current conference rules) rather than a malicious exploit against a defended system. The paper's concern is academic-integrity/fairness and algorithmic monoculture, not confidentiality/integrity/availability compromise of an AI system.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (detailed in Appendix A, not fully retrieved in this pass): use of a single/limited prompt set for AI reviewers, and reliance on third-party (Pangram/EditLens) labels to detect AI-generated reviews in the wild, which may be imperfect. The laundering experiment used only 60 sampled papers and a specific set of launderer/reviewer LLMs (GPT-5.1, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.5), so generalization to other model families or future model generations is untested. The paper is explicitly a position paper with a normative call to action, not a technical defense or detection method; it proposes evaluation requirements (adversarial robustness testing, false-positive-rate validation, transparency) rather than solving the gaming problem itself.

datasets_used: 75,800 ICLR 2026 reviews from 19,490 papers, with AI-generation labels from Emi (2025)/EditLens (Thai et al., 2025)
60 randomly sampled ICLR 2026 papers reviewed by AI reviewer agents (Bianchi et al., 2025b implementation)
8,015 ICLR 2026 papers with at least one human and one AI review (for AUC comparison)
6,903 pairwise paper-embedding comparisons among the 60 sampled papers (original vs. laundered)
quantitative_results: InterSim in the wild: fully AI-generated reviews mean 0.486 vs. other reviews mean 0.467 (Welch's t=3218, p<0.0001, Cohen's d=0.29)
Simulated IntraSim: ICLR human mean 0.811; AI reviews of original papers mean 0.882 (+8.7%, d=1.47); AI reviews of laundered papers mean 0.891 (+9.8%, d=1.67)

Simulated InterSim: ICLR human mean 0.470; GPT-5.1 original +37.4% (mean 0.646, d=3.55), GPT-5.1 laundered +39.8% (mean 0.657, d=3.79); Claude original +17.6% (mean 0.553, d=1.41), Claude laundered +20.0% (mean 0.564, d=1.62)
AI vs. human score correlation: AI-human Pearson r=0.15; AI-AI r=0.49
AI score inflation: mean 7.3 (GPT) and 6.1 (Claude) vs. 4.3 (human), on presumed 1-10 scale
Prediction of accept/reject: averaged human scores AUC=0.822 vs. averaged AI scores AUC=0.710
Paper laundering overall mean score increase across 24 conditions: +0.45 points (1-10 scale), Wilcoxon p<0.001 in nearly every condition

Common AI reviewer boilerplate phrases: GPT phrase 'if not, can you comment on' appears in 13.3% of papers; Claude phrase 'how does the method handle' appears in 21.7% of papers; most common ICLR human phrases appear in <1% of papers
Paper-embedding similarity after laundering: mean 0.497 (original) to 0.529 (laundered), +6.5%, Cohen's d=1.02, t=84.8, p<0.0001
Restricting to weaknesses/questions sections: IntraSim Cohen's d increases from 1.47 to 1.93 (original) and 1.67 to 2.29 (laundered); in-the-wild InterSim effect increases to Cohen's d=0.35
baselines_compared: Human ICLR 2026 reviewers (real reviews) as the comparison baseline for both diversity metrics and score-based accept/reject prediction
AI reviewer agents: GPT-5.1, GPT-5.4, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 as both reviewer models and (GPT-5.1/GPT-5.4) as launderer models

Prior reported AI-AI review score correlation of 0.48 from Bianchi et al. (2025b), cited as consistent with this paper's own r=0.49 finding (this is a comparison to cited prior work, not a baseline system the authors reimplemented)
